evidence: Top-level project structure (CLI/, Web/, MCP/, Dockerfiles/) maps cleanly to domain roles — CLI engine, web frontend, MCP integration, Docker worker images.
Within CLI/multivol/, api_server/ is a coherent sub-package for the Flask REST layer, with config, database, auth, utils, and routes/ correctly separated.

CLI/multivol/api_strings_extension.py uses bare @app.route decorators (no Flask app defined or imported in the file) and references DB_PATH and send_file which are also undefined — the file is structurally inert and cannot run. It lives at the CLI orchestration layer rather than in api_server/routes/ where it belongs.

CLI/multivol/api_server/__init__.py creates `api_blueprint = Blueprint('api', __name__)` and then imports all route blueprints, but this api_blueprint is never registered to any Flask app. app.py independently re-imports all the same blueprints and registers them directly. The __init__.py object is a phantom that adds noise without serving a role.

resolve_path() is duplicated identically in multi_volatility2.py (line 15) and multi_volatility3.py (line 17), and a third variant resolve_host_path() lives in api_server/utils.py. Three implementations of the same host-path translation logic exist with no shared owner.
multivol/__init__.py is entirely empty — it offers no public surface and contributes nothing to the package contract.

strengths: Top-level monorepo split (CLI/, Web/, MCP/, Dockerfiles/) maps directly to domain capabilities — a new engineer can explain each directory's role at a glance.
api_server/ is internally coherent: config, database, auth middleware, utils, and routes/ as a Blueprint sub-package is a recognizable and predictable Flask layout.
Blueprint registration in app.py is explicit and centralised — all route ownership is visible in one place.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in the multivol package layer where dead/orphaned code (api_strings_extension.py), a phantom object (api_blueprint in __init__.py), and duplicated cross-cutting logic (resolve_path triplicated) blur boundaries and mislead engineers about where capabilities live and who owns them.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The coarse decomposition (CLI engine / web / Docker workers) is sound and trustworthy. However, within the CLI package a new engineer would be confused by api_strings_extension.py (inert Flask routes at the wrong layer with undefined symbols), the api_blueprint created in __init__.py that is never used, and path-translation logic duplicated three times with no clear owner. These are not isolated nits — they represent ownership ambiguity for an active concern (Docker volume path resolution) and a dead surface that implies missing functionality. This places the codebase in the 60-70 band: overall structure is navigable but the package internals have enough misleading artifacts that a developer cannot fully trust the decomposition signals.

issues.summary: api_strings_extension.py contains inert Flask routes at the wrong layer with undefined symbols
evidence: File uses @app.route decorators but no Flask app object is created or imported — file cannot be executed.
References sqlite3.connect(DB_PATH) and send_file without importing or defining either.
File lives in CLI/multivol/ (orchestration layer) rather than CLI/multivol/api_server/routes/ where route Blueprints belong.
Not imported anywhere in the package — confirmed by grep across CLI/.
issues.suggestion: Convert the two route functions to a Blueprint (e.g. strings_bp) and move the file to CLI/multivol/api_server/routes/strings.py, replacing the bare sqlite3 calls with get_db_connection() from api_server/database.py. Register the blueprint in app.py. If the functionality is intentionally deferred, delete the file to avoid misleading future engineers.

issues.summary: api_server/__init__.py creates an api_blueprint that is never registered and double-imports all route blueprints
evidence: __init__.py: `api_blueprint = Blueprint('api', __name__)` — object created but never passed to app.register_blueprint().

__init__.py imports files_bp, docker_bp, scan_bp, dump_bp, memprocfs_bp; app.py independently imports the same five blueprints plus auth_bp and registers them. Both imports are live but __init__.py's serve no functional purpose.
A new engineer reading __init__.py would expect api_blueprint to aggregate the sub-blueprints, but no such aggregation occurs.
issues.suggestion: Either (a) remove the api_blueprint creation and the blueprint imports from __init__.py entirely, leaving only `from .app import run_api` (the only useful export), or (b) use api_blueprint as the actual aggregator: register child blueprints onto it with url_prefix and register only api_blueprint in app.py, eliminating the duplicate import chain.

issues.summary: Docker host-path translation logic is duplicated identically in three places with no shared owner
evidence: multi_volatility2.py line 15 and multi_volatility3.py line 17 define structurally identical resolve_path(self, path, host_path) methods — both handle /storage prefix, BASE_DIR prefix, and os.getcwd() fallback.
api_server/utils.py line 9 defines resolve_host_path(container_path) which performs the same host-path translation using HOST_PATH env var.
No single module owns path resolution — ownership is split across orchestration classes and utility module.
issues.suggestion: Extract one canonical resolve_host_path(path, host_base=None) function into CLI/multivol/api_server/utils.py (or a new CLI/multivol/docker_utils.py if CLI-layer access to api_server feels wrong). Have both multi_volatility2 and multi_volatility3 import and call it instead of defining their own methods.

root_causes: Incremental feature addition without enforcing Blueprint conventions — routes added at the wrong layer (api_strings_extension.py) rather than following the established pattern in api_server/routes/.
Unfinished refactor: api_blueprint suggests an intent to group blueprints under a parent, but the refactor was abandoned mid-way, leaving a phantom object.

Parallel class hierarchy (multi_volatility2, multi_volatility3) duplicates cross-cutting logic instead of sharing a common base or utility, because no shared utility layer was established before both classes were written.
